edges.description: The expanded DMPK allele is transcribed into CUG repeat-containing RNA that accumulates in nuclear foci.
edges.source: DMPK CTG Repeat Expansion

edges.description: The repeat expansion sits adjacent to SIX5, supporting a parallel locus-dosage branch for cataract susceptibility.
intermediate_mechanisms: Repeat expansion near SIX5 may alter local chromatin and SIX5 transcription.
edges.source: DMPK CTG Repeat Expansion

edges.description: Expanded CUG RNA foci sequester MBNL proteins and shift CELF1/MBNL balance, producing widespread developmental splice reversion.
edges.source: Toxic CUG RNA Nuclear Foci
edges.target: Splicing Factor Imbalance and Fetal Spliceopathy

edges.description: MBNL1 depletion causes CLCN1 mis-splicing, loss of functional ClC-1 chloride channel, and skeletal muscle hyperexcitability.
edges.source: Splicing Factor Imbalance and Fetal Spliceopathy
edges.target: CLCN1 Mis-splicing and Reduced Chloride Conductance

edges.description: Mis-splicing of CaV1.1/CACNA1S alters excitation-contraction coupling in skeletal muscle and contributes to weakness.
edges.source: Splicing Factor Imbalance and Fetal Spliceopathy
edges.target: CACNA1S Mis-splicing and Excitation-Contraction Coupling Defect

edges.description: Oropharyngeal skeletal muscle spliceopathy contributes to swallowing dysfunction in DM1.
edges.source: Splicing Factor Imbalance and Fetal Spliceopathy

edges.description: Aberrant insulin receptor splicing favors the lower-signaling IR-A isoform in skeletal muscle.
edges.source: Splicing Factor Imbalance and Fetal Spliceopathy
edges.target: INSR Splicing Shift and Insulin Resistance

edges.description: Cardiac spliceopathy shifts SCN5A toward a fetal exon 6A isoform with reduced excitability.
edges.source: Splicing Factor Imbalance and Fetal Spliceopathy
edges.target: SCN5A Splicing Shift and Cardiac Excitability Loss

edges.description: MBNL1 sequestration drives BIN1 exon 11 mis-splicing, producing an inactive BIN1 isoform that disrupts T-tubule biogenesis and excitation-contraction coupling.
edges.source: Splicing Factor Imbalance and Fetal Spliceopathy

edges.description: MBNL1/2 sequestration in the CNS alters brain splicing programs, including MAPT/tau-related transcripts.
edges.source: Splicing Factor Imbalance and Fetal Spliceopathy

edges.description: Loss of functional ClC-1 chloride conductance causes the muscle hyperexcitability phenotype.
edges.source: CLCN1 Mis-splicing and Reduced Chloride Conductance

edges.description: Altered CaV1.1 gating impairs excitation-contraction coupling and contributes to progressive skeletal muscle weakness.
edges.source: CACNA1S Mis-splicing and Excitation-Contraction Coupling Defect

edges.description: Excitation-contraction coupling defects and myopathic changes can contribute to fatigue in DM1.
intermediate_mechanisms: Skeletal muscle spliceopathy contributes to impaired muscle performance and reduced activity tolerance.
edges.source: CACNA1S Mis-splicing and Excitation-Contraction Coupling Defect

edges.description: The same skeletal muscle weakness branch can involve respiratory muscles and worsen ventilatory capacity.
intermediate_mechanisms: Weakness of respiratory muscles contributes to restrictive ventilatory impairment.
edges.source: CACNA1S Mis-splicing and Excitation-Contraction Coupling Defect
edges.target: Respiratory Muscle and Central Drive Dysfunction

edges.description: Predominant IR-A expression lowers insulin signaling in skeletal muscle and contributes to glucose intolerance and diabetes.
edges.source: INSR Splicing Shift and Insulin Resistance

edges.description: Reduced excitability of the fetal SCN5A/Nav1.5 isoform contributes to conduction delay and arrhythmia.
edges.source: SCN5A Splicing Shift and Cardiac Excitability Loss

edges.description: BIN1-driven T-tubule disruption impairs excitation-contraction coupling and contributes to progressive DM1 muscle weakness.
edges.source: BIN1 Mis-splicing and T-tubule Disruption

edges.description: CNS spliceopathy and tau dysregulation provide a mechanistic basis for cognitive impairment in DM1.
edges.source: CNS Spliceopathy and Tau Dysregulation

edges.description: CNS MBNL sequestration and aberrant brain splicing contribute to hypersomnia in DM1.
edges.source: CNS Spliceopathy and Tau Dysregulation

edges.description: CNS MBNL sequestration and disrupted sleep-wake circuitry contribute to sleep fragmentation and insomnia in DM1.
edges.source: CNS Spliceopathy and Tau Dysregulation

edges.description: DMPK/SIX5 locus effects provide a mutation-to-lens opacity path.
intermediate_mechanisms: Reduced SIX5 dosage alters lens ion homeostasis and promotes progressive cataract formation.
edges.source: SIX5/DMPK Locus Effects in Lens

edges.description: Restrictive ventilatory impairment and hypoventilation manifest clinically as respiratory insufficiency.
edges.source: Respiratory Muscle and Central Drive Dysfunction
Pharmacotherapy for Myotonia
edges.target: CLCN1 Mis-splicing and Reduced Chloride Conductance

edges.source: DMPK

nodes.description: MBNL1 sequestration in DM1 causes mis-splicing of bridging integrator-1 (BIN1) pre-mRNA in skeletal muscle, producing an inactive BIN1 isoform lacking phosphatidylinositol 5-phosphate-binding and membrane- tubulating activities. T-tubule biogenesis is impaired, disrupting excitation-contraction coupling and contributing to the muscle weakness that is distinct from myotonia (driven by CLCN1).

evidence.reference_title: Misregulated alternative splicing of BIN1 is associated with T tubule alterations and muscle weakness in myotonic dystrophy.
evidence.snippet: BIN1 missplicing results in expression of an inactive form of BIN1 lacking phosphatidylinositol 5-phosphate-binding and membrane-tubulating activities.

nodes.description: DM1 spliceopathy represses inclusion of CACNA1S/CaV1.1 exon 29 in skeletal muscle. CaV1.1 controls excitation-contraction coupling, so this splice shift changes calcium-channel gating and contributes to progressive muscle weakness and myopathic changes.
nodes.id: CACNA1S Mis-splicing and Excitation-Contraction Coupling Defect

evidence.reference_title: Muscle weakness in myotonic dystrophy associated with misregulated splicing and altered gating of Ca(V)1.1 calcium channel.
evidence.snippet: The extent of E29 skipping correlated with severity of weakness in tibialis anterior muscle of DM1 patients.

nodes.description: MBNL1 sequestration causes mis-splicing of the chloride channel gene CLCN1, leading to loss of functional ClC-1 chloride channels in skeletal muscle membranes. Reduced chloride conductance causes membrane hyperexcitability and myotonia.
nodes.id: CLCN1 Mis-splicing and Reduced Chloride Conductance

evidence.reference_title: Myotonic disorders.
evidence.snippet: Altered splice products do not encode functional ClC-1 protein. Subsequent loss of chloride conductance in muscle membrane causes myotonia in the myotonic dystrophies.

nodes.description: DM1 affects the central nervous system through MBNL1/2 sequestration and aberrant brain splicing. MAPT/tau transcript dysregulation and abnormal tau deposition provide a mechanistic bridge to cognitive and behavioral impairment.

evidence.reference_title: Differences in splicing defects between the grey and white matter in myotonic dystrophy type 1 patients.
evidence.snippet: This leads to the sequestration of splicing factors such as muscleblind-like 1/2 (MBNL1/2) and aberrant splicing in the central nervous system.

evidence.reference_title: Neuropathology of classic myotonic dystrophy type 1 is characterized by both early initiation of primary age-related tauopathy of the hippocampus and unique 3-repeat tauopathy of the brainstem.
evidence.snippet: Myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1) is an inherited autosomal-dominant condition that induces altered splicing of transcripts, including MAPT, leading to a distinctive abnormal deposition of tau protein in the CNS.

nodes.description: Cardiac involvement includes conduction abnormalities (first-degree AV block, bundle branch block), arrhythmias, and cardiomyopathy. Cardiac complications are a leading cause of death in DM1.

evidence.reference_title: Splicing misregulation of SCN5A contributes to cardiac-conduction delay and heart arrhythmia in myotonic dystrophy.
evidence.snippet: Cardiac alterations, characterized by conduction delays and arrhythmia, are the second most common cause of death in DM.

evidence.reference_title: Steinert myotonic dystrophy (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0031546 | Cardiac conduction abnormality | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Posterior subcapsular cataracts are a characteristic finding, often present before age 50. Iridescent multicolored opacities (Christmas tree cataracts) are pathognomonic for DM1.

evidence.reference_title: Cataracts in myotonic dystrophy.
evidence.snippet: Perhaps the most noteworthy of these is the distinctive myotonic cataract which often appears early on in the course of the disorder.

evidence.reference_title: Steinert myotonic dystrophy (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0007787 | Posterior subcapsular cataract | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Central nervous system involvement includes cognitive impairment, apathy, and other behavioral manifestations in adult DM1.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical features and genetic spectrum of a multicenter Chinese cohort with myotonic dystrophy type 1.
evidence.snippet: Regarding the comorbidities, 4.7% of patients had tumors, 17.2% had diabetes, 23.4% had dyspnea, 28.1% had intermittent insomnia, 43.8% experienced dysphagia, and 25% exhibited cognitive impairment.

evidence.reference_title: Steinert myotonic dystrophy (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0100543 | Cognitive impairment | Frequent (79-30%)

evidence.reference_title: Molecular genetics of congenital myotonic dystrophy.
evidence.snippet: Myotonic Dystrophy type 1 (DM1) is a neuromuscular disease showing strong genetic anticipation, and is caused by the expansion of a CTG repeat tract in the 3'-UTR of the DMPK gene.

nodes.description: DM1 begins with an unstable CTG trinucleotide repeat expansion in the 3'-untranslated region of DMPK. The repeat-expansion allele is germline, autosomal dominant, and becomes more unstable across generations and across tissues, providing the initiating mutation for downstream RNA toxicity.

evidence.reference_title: Molecular genetics of congenital myotonic dystrophy.
evidence.snippet: Myotonic Dystrophy type 1 (DM1) is a neuromuscular disease showing strong genetic anticipation, and is caused by the expansion of a CTG repeat tract in the 3'-UTR of the DMPK gene.

evidence.reference_title: Myotonic dystrophy: RNA pathogenesis comes into focus.
evidence.snippet: In 1992, the mutation responsible for DM1 was identified as a CTG expansion located in the 3' untranslated region of the dystrophia myotonica-protein kinase gene (DMPK).

nodes.description: Swallowing difficulty is a frequent multisystem manifestation of DM1 and can require nutritional support in advanced cases.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical features and genetic spectrum of a multicenter Chinese cohort with myotonic dystrophy type 1.
evidence.snippet: Regarding the comorbidities, 4.7% of patients had tumors, 17.2% had diabetes, 23.4% had dyspnea, 28.1% had intermittent insomnia, 43.8% experienced dysphagia, and 25% exhibited cognitive impairment.

nodes.description: Excessive daytime sleepiness is common in DM1 and can substantially affect daily functioning.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical features and genetic spectrum of a multicenter Chinese cohort with myotonic dystrophy type 1.
evidence.snippet: Of them, 70.3% of patients had daytime sleepiness, 14.1% had cataract surgery, 7.8% used wheelchairs, 4.7% required ventilatory support, and 1.6% required gastric tubes.

evidence.reference_title: Steinert myotonic dystrophy (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0001262 | Excessive daytime somnolence | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Fatigue is among the most common complaints in DM1, affecting the majority of patients and substantially impairing quality of life and daily activity.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical features and genetic spectrum of a multicenter Chinese cohort with myotonic dystrophy type 1.
evidence.snippet: Muscle weakness (92.2%), myotonia (85.9%), and fatigue (73.4%) were the most prevalent clinical features.

evidence.reference_title: Steinert myotonic dystrophy (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0012378 | Fatigue | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Aberrant splicing of insulin receptor pre-mRNA in DM1 skeletal muscle favors the lower-signaling IR-A isoform, reducing metabolic insulin responsiveness and contributing to insulin resistance and diabetes mellitus.
nodes.id: INSR Splicing Shift and Insulin Resistance

evidence.reference_title: Aberrant regulation of insulin receptor alternative splicing is associated with insulin resistance in myotonic dystrophy.
evidence.snippet: alternative splicing of the insulin receptor (IR) pre-mRNA is aberrantly regulated in DM1 skeletal muscle tissue, resulting in predominant expression of the lower-signaling nonmuscle isoform (IR-A).

nodes.description: Intermittent insomnia has been documented in DM1, contributing to overall sleep disturbance alongside excessive daytime somnolence.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical features and genetic spectrum of a multicenter Chinese cohort with myotonic dystrophy type 1.
evidence.snippet: Regarding the comorbidities, 4.7% of patients had tumors, 17.2% had diabetes, 23.4% had dyspnea, 28.1% had intermittent insomnia, 43.8% experienced dysphagia, and 25% exhibited cognitive impairment.

nodes.description: Endocrine manifestations include insulin resistance, diabetes mellitus, hypogonadism in males, and thyroid dysfunction.

evidence.reference_title: Aberrant regulation of insulin receptor alternative splicing is associated with insulin resistance in myotonic dystrophy.
evidence.snippet: People with DM1 have an unusual form of insulin resistance caused by a defect in skeletal muscle.

evidence.reference_title: Steinert myotonic dystrophy (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0000819 | Diabetes mellitus | Occasional (29-5%)

nodes.description: Myotonia, the hallmark symptom, is characterized by delayed relaxation of skeletal muscle after contraction. Patients describe stiffness that improves with repeated muscle contractions (warm-up phenomenon).

evidence.reference_title: Myotonic disorders.
evidence.snippet: Myotonia reflects a state of muscle fiber hyperexcitability. Impaired transmembrane conductance of either chloride or sodium ions results in myotonia.

evidence.reference_title: Steinert myotonic dystrophy (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0003740 | Myotonia with warm-up phenomenon | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Mexiletine is the most commonly used antimyotonia agent. Other options include phenytoin and carbamazepine for symptomatic relief of myotonia.
Progressive skeletal muscle weakness and wasting, particularly affecting distal muscles initially (grip, ankle dorsiflexors), with later involvement of proximal muscles and facial muscles.

evidence.reference_title: Myotonic dystrophy: candidate small molecule therapeutics.
evidence.snippet: Myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1) is a rare multisystemic neuromuscular disorder caused by expansion of CTG trinucleotide repeats in the noncoding region of the DMPK gene.

evidence.reference_title: Muscle weakness in myotonic dystrophy associated with misregulated splicing and altered gating of Ca(V)1.1 calcium channel.
evidence.snippet: The extent of E29 skipping correlated with severity of weakness in tibialis anterior muscle of DM1 patients.

evidence.reference_title: Steinert myotonic dystrophy (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0002460 | Distal muscle weakness | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Respiratory failure from weakness of the diaphragm and intercostal muscles is a major cause of morbidity and mortality.

evidence.reference_title: Respiratory dysfunction in myotonic dystrophy type 1: A systematic review.
evidence.snippet: respiratory dysfunction, predominantly a restrictive ventilatory pattern, is common in myotonic dystrophy and is associated with alveolar hypoventilation, chronic hypercapnia, and sleep disturbance in the form of sleep apnoea and sleep related disordered breathing;

evidence.reference_title: Steinert myotonic dystrophy (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0002093 | Respiratory insufficiency | Occasional (29-5%)

nodes.description: Respiratory morbidity in DM1 reflects both peripheral respiratory muscle weakness and central respiratory drive involvement. The result is commonly restrictive ventilatory dysfunction with alveolar hypoventilation, chronic hypercapnia, and sleep-disordered breathing.
nodes.id: Respiratory Muscle and Central Drive Dysfunction

evidence.reference_title: Respiratory dysfunction in myotonic dystrophy type 1: A systematic review.
evidence.snippet: respiratory dysfunction, predominantly a restrictive ventilatory pattern, is common in myotonic dystrophy and is associated with alveolar hypoventilation, chronic hypercapnia, and sleep disturbance in the form of sleep apnoea and sleep related disordered breathing;

nodes.description: In DM1 heart tissue, MBNL-dependent spliceopathy shifts SCN5A from the adult exon 6B isoform toward the fetal exon 6A isoform. The resulting Nav1.5 channel has reduced excitability, providing a mutation-to-cardiac conduction defect branch in the pathograph.
nodes.id: SCN5A Splicing Shift and Cardiac Excitability Loss

evidence.reference_title: Splicing misregulation of SCN5A contributes to cardiac-conduction delay and heart arrhythmia in myotonic dystrophy.
evidence.snippet: Using RNA sequencing, here we identify novel splicing alterations in DM heart samples, including a switch from adult exon 6B towards fetal exon 6A in the cardiac sodium channel, SCN5A.

nodes.description: The DM1 CTG repeat lies near SIX5, and repeat-associated local chromatin or transcriptional effects can reduce SIX5 dosage. Mouse Six5 loss produces progressive ocular cataracts, supporting a parallel lens branch in addition to toxic RNA spliceopathy.

evidence.reference_title: Heterozygous loss of Six5 in mice is sufficient to cause ocular cataracts.
evidence.snippet: Our results demonstrate that the rate and severity of cataract formation is inversely related to Six5 dosage and is temporally progressive.

nodes.description: Expanded CUG RNA sequesters MBNL family proteins in nuclear foci and is accompanied by increased CELF1/CUGBP1 activity in affected tissues. The resulting loss of adult MBNL activity and CELF1/MBNL imbalance causes fetal-pattern alternative splicing across skeletal muscle, heart, endocrine, and CNS transcripts.
nodes.id: Splicing Factor Imbalance and Fetal Spliceopathy

evidence.reference_title: Myotonic disorders.
evidence.snippet: Mutant RNA sequesters MBNL1, a splice regulator protein and depletes MBNL1 from the nucleoplasm. Loss of MBNL1 results in altered splicing of ClC-1 mRNA.

evidence.reference_title: Splicing misregulation of SCN5A contributes to cardiac-conduction delay and heart arrhythmia in myotonic dystrophy.
evidence.snippet: The pathogenesis of DM involves a RNA gain-of-function mechanism caused by expression of mutant RNAs containing hundred to thousands of CUG or CCUG repeats that interfere with the splicing of other pre-mRNAs through dysfunction of two classes of RNA-binding proteins.

nodes.description: The expanded DMPK repeat is transcribed into CUG repeat-containing RNA that forms nuclear foci. These foci are the central toxic RNA species in DM1, retaining mutant RNA in the nucleus and recruiting RNA-binding proteins that normally regulate adult splicing programs.

evidence.reference_title: Myotonic syndromes.
evidence.snippet: In both types of myotonic dystrophy the expanded repeat is transcribed and the RNA produced from the mutant allele is retained in nuclear inclusions. Recent studies suggest that the mutant RNA has a toxic effect on muscle fibers by interfering with the essential functions of the myonucleus, such as RNA processing.

evidence.reference_title: Myotonic dystrophy: RNA pathogenesis comes into focus.
evidence.snippet: the clinical features common to both diseases are caused by a gain-of-function RNA mechanism in which the CUG and CCUG repeats alter cellular function, including alternative splicing of various genes.
